primary_diagnosis.name: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) — acute flare, moderate severity

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic relapsing intensely pruritic dry rash — the classic atopic pattern.
Itchy dermatitis could reflect an external contact trigger — needs distribution and exposure history to sort out.
Severe generalised itch worse at night raises scabies — must screen distribution (finger webs, wrists, genitals) and burrows.
A dermatological emergency that complicates eczema — must be actively screened for (monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, pain, fever) even though it looks unlikely.
Symmetrical flexural distribution, chronic xerosis, lichenification, personal atopy (allergic rhinitis) and an environmental (cold/dry) trigger — textbook endogenous atopic eczema.
Symmetrical flexural pattern argues against an exogenous contact cause, which usually maps to the contact site; kept as a lesser consideration.
Chronic history, flexural (not finger-web/genital) distribution and no burrows make scabies unlikely — reconsider if standard therapy fails.
No fever, no punched-out vesicles/erosions, no honey-coloured crusts or weeping — eczema herpeticum and secondary bacterial superinfection screened negative.
No honey-coloured crusting, pustules or weeping — not currently infected, but a real risk given the excoriations.
No monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, no pain out of proportion, no systemic features — excluded on today's screen but the key safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: atopic dermatitis (eczema), moderate flare, no superinfection
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed classic atopic flare driven by an environmental trigger and an impaired skin barrier; telehealth-manageable with topical therapy and safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Eczema herpeticum — clustered monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, painful skin, malaise/fever (disseminated HSV on eczematous skin)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency; can disseminate and cause serious systemic illness. Needs urgent review and systemic aciclovir.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to widespread disseminated HSV, ocular involvement, or systemic sepsis if treated as ordinary eczema.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial (staphylococcal) superinfection — honey-coloured crusts, weeping yellow fluid, pustules, spreading redness, fever, skin hot to touch
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated eczema is prone to Staph superinfection; needs topical/oral antibiotics and same-day review if it develops.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis/impetiginisation, worsening flare and systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Extensive excoriation to the point of bleeding with severe (48-hour) sleep deprivation and marked distress
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks flare severity and the urgency of breaking the itch-scratch cycle; not itself life-threatening but drives the need for prompt effective therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing skin breakdown (portal for infection), worsening sleep loss and psychological exhaustion.

pathophysiology_summary: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disease driven by a defective epidermal barrier (filaggrin dysfunction, reduced ceramides) and a Th2-skewed immune response. The impaired barrier increases transepidermal water loss (xerosis) and admits irritants and allergens, provoking pruritus; scratching further damages the barrier, producing the self-perpetuating itch-scratch cycle, excoriation and, over time, lichenification. Flares are commonly triggered by low humidity/cold dry air, irritants (soaps, fragrances) and stress. This patient has the atopic diathesis (allergic rhinitis), classic flexural involvement and an environmental trigger. Management restores the barrier (liberal fragrance-free emollients, soap avoidance) and suppresses inflammation (a time-limited course of an appropriate-potency topical corticosteroid), with a sedating antihistamine used only to aid sleep and reduce nocturnal scratching. The differentials that must not be missed are eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV) and secondary bacterial superinfection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being anchored by the patient's distress and sleep deprivation (or the source note's 'urgent after-hours' framing) into escalating a common, telehealth-manageable eczema flare to ED/000. The correct move is to treat and safety-net.
Failing to screen for and safety-net the monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions, pain and fever of eczema herpeticum — the one emergency hiding behind an eczema flare.
Prescribing antibiotics for excoriated but non-infected eczema (no honey crusts, weeping or fever) — an error of commission that drives resistance and does not treat the flare.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Emollient without steroid, or steroid without emollient
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating an active inflamed flare with moisturiser alone (under-treats inflammation) or with steroid alone (fails to repair the barrier and prevent relapse) — both halves are needed.

Either withholding an adequate-potency topical corticosteroid so the flare is under-treated, or applying a potent steroid to large areas/thin skin for too long — the balance is a short, targeted course to active patches.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal dermatological examination and precise morphology/distribution mapping
Definitive exclusion of early eczema herpeticum or subtle secondary infection without clear imaging/photos or in-person review
Auscultation of the chest (documented clear in the source in-person note)
Clinician-measured vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2, temperature)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage remotely with topical corticosteroid + liberal fragrance-free emollient + a sedating antihistamine for sleep, and give explicit safety-net triggers. Escalate only if red-flag mimics appear (eczema herpeticum → ED; bacterial superinfection → urgent same-day).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the underlying diagnosis (classic flexural atopic pattern). The real telehealth risks are OVER-action (escalating a benign flare) and failing to safety-net the two mimics that genuinely cannot be fully excluded on video.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Barrier repair with liberal fragrance-free emollients plus a time-limited topical corticosteroid of potency matched to site and severity; avoid soaps and known irritants; safety-net for eczema herpeticum and bacterial superinfection.
